content:
First, introduce coordinates.  Let \(O=(0,0)\), let the larger circle \(C_2\) have radius \(9\), and place the tangency point \(A\) at \((9,0)\).  Then the smaller circle \(C_1\) has centre \(O_1=(9-r,0)\) and radius \(r\), with \(0<r<9\).

We look for the point \(P\) on \(C_1\) such that the tangent at \(P\) passes through \(O\).  Equivalently, in the right‐triangle \(OO_1P\), the right angle is at \(P\), so
\[
OP^2+O_1P^2=OO_1^2,
\]
but \(O_1P=r\) and \(OO_1=9-r\), so
\[
OP=\sqrt{(9-r)^2-r^2}
   =\sqrt{81-18r} \;=\;3\sqrt{9-2r}.
\]
Parametrizing \(P\) on the ray making some angle \(\theta\) with the \(x\)-axis one finds
\[
\cos\theta=\frac{OP}{9-r}
            =\frac{3\sqrt{9-2r}}{9-r}, 
\quad
\sin\theta=\frac r{9-r}.
\]
The ray \(OP\) meets the big circle again at 
\[
Q\;:\;OQ=9,
\]
and meets the common tangent at \(A\) (the vertical line \(x=9\)) at
\[
R\;:\;OR=\frac{9}{\cos\theta}
       =\frac{9(9-r)}{3\sqrt{9-2r}}.
\]
We require 
\[
PQ=QR\,,
\]
i.e.\ 
\[
9-OP \;=\;OR-9.
\]
Substituting \(OP=3\sqrt{9-2r}\) and \(OR\) and clearing denominators leads to the quadratic
\[
(\sqrt{9-2r}-1)(\sqrt{9-2r}-3)=0.
\]
The admissible root is \(\sqrt{9-2r}=1\), giving \(r=4\) and hence
\[
OP=3\sqrt{9-2\cdot4}=3\sqrt1=3.
\]
Answer: \(\boxed{3}\).
